questions.questionText: Ovulation is triggered by a surge in which hormone?
questions.explanation: The LH surge, caused by positive feedback from estrogen, induces ovulation.

questions.questionText: The primary source of progesterone during the secretory phase of the menstrual cycle is the:
questions.explanation: After ovulation, the remnant follicle becomes the corpus luteum and secretes progesterone.
Spermatogenesis occurs in the seminiferous tubules of the testes.

questions.questionText: Testosterone is secreted by which cells in the testes?
questions.explanation: Leydig (interstitial) cells secrete testosterone in response to LH.

questions.questionText: Sertoli cells are stimulated primarily by:
questions.explanation: FSH acts on Sertoli cells to support spermatogenesis and secrete inhibin.
The ampulla is the widest part of the tube where sperm typically meets the egg.

questions.questionText: The hormone primarily responsible for milk production (lactogenesis) is:
questions.explanation: Prolactin stimulates milk synthesis, while oxytocin stimulates let-down.

questions.questionText: The hormone primarily responsible for milk ejection (let-down reflex) is:
questions.explanation: Oxytocin causes contraction of myoepithelial cells in the breast.

questions.optionA: Low FSH and LH, low estrogen
questions.optionC: High FSH and LH, low estrogen
questions.explanation: Loss of ovarian follicles removes negative feedback, leading to elevated gonadotropins.

questions.questionText: The acrosome reaction is necessary for:
questions.optionD: Sperm penetration of the zona pellucida
questions.explanation: Enzymes released from the acrosome allow the sperm to digest the zona pellucida.

questions.questionText: In Reproductive Physiology, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Physiology texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Physiology references for Reproductive Physiology. Subject: Physiology. Chapter: Reproductive Physiology.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Reproductive Physiology scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Physiology. Chapter: Reproductive Physiology.
